intro: AI dominates this week's digest from multiple angles: OpenAI is restructuring around enterprise and shedding consumer moonshots, Anthropic's Claude Mythos model is alarming governments and the financial world, and Sam Altman's World identity project is quietly embedding itself everywhere from Tinder to Zoom.

stories.title: OpenAI Shuts Down Sora, Folds Science Team as Kevin Weil and Bill Peebles Exit
stories.summary: OpenAI is in the middle of a significant strategic pivot, and the departures of Chief Product Officer Kevin Weil and researcher Bill Peebles are the clearest signal yet. The company is shutting down Sora, its AI video generation product, and folding its AI-for-science team into the Codex organization — framing it as shedding 'side quests' to focus on enterprise AI. Weil, a former Instagram VP, joined OpenAI to build consumer products; the fact that he's leaving as those bets get unwound is pointed. Peebles was a key figure behind Sora's technical development, so his departure suggests this isn't just a product repositioning but a genuine loss of momentum in generative video. The pivot toward enterprise is unsurprising given where the money is, but it does raise questions about OpenAI's longer-term relevance in consumer AI as competitors like Google and Anthropic continue investing broadly. The Codex absorption of the science team is particularly interesting — it signals OpenAI sees AI-assisted software development as its core near-term enterprise wedge. OpenAI recently updated Codex significantly, adding computer use, in-app browsing, image generation, memory, and plugins. The company also launched GPT-Rosalind for life sciences research and expanded its cybersecurity Trusted Access program, suggesting it's not abandoning specialized verticals entirely — just consolidating them under a more enterprise-coherent umbrella. For engineers watching this space: OpenAI is increasingly looking like a B2B infrastructure company with a ChatGPT consumer brand attached, rather than a broad AI lab swinging for moonshots. Whether that's the right call or a loss of ambition is genuinely debatable.

stories.title: Anthropic's Claude Mythos Triggers White House Meeting and Financial Alarm Bells
stories.summary: Anthropic's newest model, Claude Mythos, has caused enough concern that the White House held what was described as a 'productive' meeting with the company — a notable sign that the US government sees Anthropic's technology as too strategically critical to alienate, even amid concerns about the model's capabilities. BBC's reporting highlights that Mythos can outperform humans at some hacking and cybersecurity tasks, which has specifically spooked the financial sector about potential misuse in attacks on banking and trading infrastructure. This is a different kind of model-release story than the usual benchmark announcements — it's about a capability threshold that regulators and national security officials are actively trying to get their heads around. The context of AI in warfare matters here: MIT Technology Review ran a detailed piece this week on why 'humans in the loop' in AI-driven conflict is increasingly an illusion, and Mythos's cybersecurity capabilities fit uncomfortably into that picture. Anthropic's positioning has always been that it's the 'responsible' AI lab, which is partly why the White House engagement is framed cooperatively rather than adversarially. But the company is now navigating real tension between publishing frontier capabilities and managing the geopolitical consequences. The Pentagon relationship is also complicated — there's an ongoing legal dispute between Anthropic and the DoD referenced in the MIT piece. For security engineers and those in financial services, Mythos's offensive cybersecurity capabilities warrant close attention even if you never use Anthropic's products directly, since it raises the baseline of what sophisticated attackers can access.

stories.title: Sam Altman's World Expands Human Verification to Tinder and Zoom
stories.summary: World — the Sam Altman-backed project formerly known as Worldcoin that scans irises with its 'Orb' device to create a cryptographic proof of human identity — is making a major expansion push, landing partnerships with both Tinder and Zoom. Tinder will show a verification badge for users who've proven they're human via World's system, an obvious response to the proliferation of AI-generated profiles and chatbots on dating platforms. Zoom's integration is arguably more consequential for the enterprise: meeting participants can now display a 'verified human' badge on their tile, addressing growing anxiety about AI-generated video imposters in video calls. The underlying technology ties iris biometric data to an anonymous credential, so in theory you're proving humanness without revealing identity — but 'in theory' is doing a lot of work there, and privacy advocates have been raising eyebrows at World for years. What's notable is the breadth of World's expansion: TechCrunch reports a 'bevy of new partnerships' beyond just these two, suggesting Altman is treating World as a foundational identity layer for the internet rather than a standalone product. This is a genuinely important infrastructure play if it works — the ability to cryptographically distinguish humans from AI agents is going to matter enormously as agentic AI proliferates. The question is whether you trust a private company with a financial token attached to be that infrastructure. The Orb requirement also creates real friction — not everyone can or will go get their iris scanned. Still, the momentum here is real and the problem World is trying to solve is real, even if the solution remains controversial.

stories.title: OpenAI Launches GPT-Rosalind for Life Sciences and Expands Cyber Defense Program
stories.summary: Amid the strategic reshuffling, OpenAI this week quietly shipped two significant products. GPT-Rosalind is a frontier reasoning model purpose-built for drug discovery, genomics analysis, protein reasoning, and scientific research workflows — named after Rosalind Franklin, the crystallographer whose work was central to understanding DNA structure. This is OpenAI's clearest bet yet on AI-accelerated science as a commercial vertical, and it positions them directly against specialized biotech AI players. Separately, OpenAI expanded its Trusted Access for Cyber program, which now includes leading security firms getting access to GPT-5.4-Cyber along with $10M in API grants for defensive cybersecurity work. The dual nature of this — releasing a powerful cybersecurity model while simultaneously running a vetting program for who gets access — reflects the same tension visible in the Anthropic/Mythos story. OpenAI is trying to be the responsible actor by creating a controlled access tier, but that only works if the vetting is genuinely rigorous. The Codex app also received a significant update, adding computer use, in-app browsing, image generation, memory, and plugins for macOS and Windows — making it a much more capable developer agent rather than just a code completion tool. For engineers, the Codex update is probably the most immediately actionable item here: if you're using it for developer workflows, the new computer use capability in particular expands what you can automate substantially. The GPT-Rosalind announcement signals that OpenAI sees specialized scientific reasoning as a distinct product category worth building for, rather than assuming general models will be good enough.

stories.title: 'Tokenmaxxing' Is Making Developers Less Productive Than They Think
stories.summary: There's a growing pattern in AI-assisted development that TechCrunch is calling 'tokenmaxxing' — essentially, developers feeding enormous amounts of context to AI models and generating massive amounts of code, but then spending disproportionate time reviewing, rewriting, and debugging that code because its quality doesn't match its volume. The core insight is uncomfortable: more tokens consumed does not equal more work done, and the economics of AI coding assistance look much worse once you account for the rewriting overhead and the cognitive load of reviewing AI-generated code you didn't write. This connects to a broader debate about where AI coding tools actually provide leverage — they're clearly useful for boilerplate, for explaining unfamiliar codebases, and for generating tests, but the 'just prompt your way to a feature' workflow may be generating technical debt faster than it's generating features. The Hacker News post 'Spending 3 months coding by hand' (160 points) is clearly adjacent to this conversation — developers are actively questioning whether AI-assisted workflows are improving their craft or degrading it. For senior engineers, this is worth taking seriously: if you're managing teams that have fully embraced AI coding tools, the output metrics (lines of code, features shipped) may be flattering while the underlying codebase quality and maintainability are deteriorating. The tokenmaxxing phenomenon also has real cost implications — API costs for frontier models are not trivial at scale, and if the code requires heavy revision anyway, the ROI math gets questionable. This doesn't mean AI coding tools aren't useful; it means the workflow optimizations that actually work are more nuanced than 'dump everything into context and accept the output.'

stories.title: Cursor in Talks to Raise $2B+ at $50B Valuation as Enterprise Adoption Surges
stories.summary: Cursor, the AI-powered code editor built on top of VS Code, is reportedly in talks to raise more than $2 billion at a $50 billion valuation, with a16z and Thrive Capital expected to lead the round. To put that number in context: $50B would make Cursor one of the most valuable private software companies in the world, full stop — and it's a code editor. The valuation reflects genuine enterprise traction, not just developer hype; the story notes that enterprise growth is surging, which means companies are paying for seat licenses at scale rather than just individual developers using the free tier. This is the AI developer tools market crystallizing around a winner, and Cursor has built a significant lead over competitors including GitHub Copilot (which has the Microsoft distribution advantage) and other AI IDE entrants. The irony of the tokenmaxxing story running the same day is not lost — Cursor is arguably the primary vehicle through which tokenmaxxing happens, yet its valuation keeps climbing because even imperfect AI coding assistance is genuinely useful enough that enterprises are paying for it at scale. a16z returning as a backer suggests they see Cursor as a durable platform rather than a feature that gets absorbed into VS Code or JetBrains. For engineers: Cursor's trajectory is worth watching not just as a tool but as a signal that the market has decided AI-native development environments are a distinct category from traditional IDEs with AI plugins bolted on. The $50B number will raise eyebrows, but the underlying enterprise adoption story appears to be real.

stories.title: Why 'Humans in the Loop' in AI Warfare Is Increasingly an Illusion
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review published a detailed analysis of why the 'human in the loop' concept — long used as a reassurance that humans retain meaningful control over AI-assisted weapons systems — is becoming functionally meaningless in modern AI-driven conflict. The piece is set against the backdrop of the ongoing conflict with Iran, where AI is now reportedly not just assisting intelligence analysis but actively participating in targeting and operational decisions at a pace that exceeds meaningful human review. The legal and ethical framework for AI in warfare was largely built around the assumption that humans would have genuine deliberative time to evaluate AI recommendations before acting on them — that assumption is breaking down as operational tempos accelerate. The Anthropic/Pentagon legal dispute mentioned in the article adds a corporate dimension: AI companies are being pulled into defense applications whether they seek it or not, and their models' capabilities are now genuinely relevant to questions of international humanitarian law. For engineers working in or adjacent to defense tech, this piece is important background for understanding why governments are suddenly very interested in AI safety from a national security angle rather than just an existential risk angle. The 'human in the loop' language is also increasingly appearing in enterprise AI governance contexts outside defense — it's worth being skeptical of it there too, since the same dynamic (AI operating faster than humans can meaningfully review) applies in high-frequency trading, fraud detection, and content moderation. This is one of those articles that reads like an important document for understanding where AI policy is heading.

stories.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS Brings Granular Audio Control to Google's Speech AI
stories.summary: Google DeepMind released Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS, a new text-to-speech model accessible via the standard Gemini API that introduces 'granular audio tags' — essentially a way to use structured prompting to control the expressiveness, pacing, and tone of AI-generated speech with fine-grained precision. This is a meaningful step beyond the current generation of TTS models where you mostly get one or two voice options and limited control over delivery. The model is available as `gemini-3.1-flash-tts-preview` in the API and outputs audio files rather than text. Simon Willison gave it a quick hands-on look and noted the audio tag system works via transcript-style tags similar to how SSML works but more LLM-native. The practical applications here are significant for anyone building voice interfaces, podcast-style content generators, accessibility tools, or interactive AI agents that need to sound natural rather than robotic. Google has been quietly building a strong audio AI portfolio — Gemini 3.1 Flash Live (for real-time voice) and now Flash TTS cover both streaming and production use cases. The 'Flash' naming convention signals this is optimized for speed and cost rather than maximum quality, which makes sense for high-volume TTS use cases. For developers building voice-forward applications, this is worth evaluating seriously — the ability to prompt-control speech characteristics rather than hardcoding voice parameters is a genuine workflow improvement. The competitive context is that ElevenLabs and others have built significant businesses on expressive TTS; Google entering with a model integrated directly into the Gemini API ecosystem will create pricing pressure.

stories.title: Republican Mutiny Kills Trump's Push to Extend Warrantless Surveillance Under Section 702
stories.summary: In a late-night surprise, a Republican revolt in the House sank the White House's effort to extend Section 702 of FISA — the surveillance authority that allows the FBI to collect communications of foreign targets without a warrant, but which has repeatedly been used to search Americans' data without a warrant as well. The FBI has documented uses of Section 702 searches against members of Congress, protesters, and political donors, which gives this particular Republican opposition an interesting self-interest dimension — some of these members may have been searched themselves. Section 702 reauthorization has been one of the most contentious recurring tech policy fights for over a decade, pitting national security hawks against civil libertarians in coalitions that don't map cleanly onto party lines. The post-midnight timing of the vote suggests the White House and party leadership thought they had the votes and didn't. For tech policy watchers: Section 702 is the legal backbone for a significant portion of US signals intelligence collection, and its lapse or significant modification would have major downstream effects on how intelligence agencies share data with allied services under frameworks like Five Eyes. The surveillance reform community has been fighting for meaningful warrant requirements for Section 702 queries on US persons for years; whether this rebellion actually produces reform or just a delayed reauthorization with cosmetic changes remains to be seen. The political dynamics are genuinely unusual — Trump-aligned Republicans voting against a surveillance expansion championed by the Trump White House suggests either principled libertarian sentiment or, more likely, specific concerns about executive power being used against political allies.

stories.title: AI Warfare's Expanding Role and the Anthropic-Pentagon Legal Dispute
stories.summary: The legal battle between Anthropic and the Pentagon has come into sharper focus this week, with reporting indicating that the dispute centers on the extent to which Anthropic's models can be used in defense and warfare applications — and under what safeguards. This sits awkwardly alongside the White House's 'productive' meeting with Anthropic over Claude Mythos, which suggests the US government is simultaneously worried about Anthropic's capabilities and unwilling to cut itself off from them. The broader context is that AI is playing an increasingly direct role in the Iran conflict, going beyond intelligence analysis into operational decision support — which makes the question of which AI companies' terms of service permit defense use highly consequential. Anthropic has tried to maintain a 'safety-first' brand identity that complicates military use cases, but the strategic importance of frontier AI capabilities means governments aren't taking no for an answer easily. For engineers at AI companies: this is the moment where 'acceptable use policies' start having real geopolitical weight rather than just being legal boilerplate. The dual-use nature of frontier AI — the same model that helps with drug discovery might outperform humans at cyberattacks — is going to force every major AI lab to have explicit positions on defense use that they can't avoid. The Anthropic situation is the most visible current example, but the underlying tension applies across the industry.

stories.title: NIST Effectively Gives Up on Enriching Most CVEs
stories.summary: A significant and underreported infrastructure story: NIST has announced it is stepping back from its role of enriching most CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) entries in the National Vulnerability Database — the authoritative public database that the entire security industry relies on to understand, prioritize, and respond to disclosed vulnerabilities. For context, NIST's NVD enrichment process takes raw CVE identifiers and adds CVSS severity scores, affected product lists, and other metadata that tools like vulnerability scanners, patch management systems, and SIEMs depend on to function. Without that enrichment, a CVE is just a number with a description. The security industry has been noticing processing backlogs at NVD for over a year; this announcement formalizes what was already becoming functionally true. The downstream effects are significant: automated vulnerability management workflows that rely on NVD data will become less reliable, and organizations will need to source enrichment from commercial vendors or other sources. Risky.biz covered this with appropriate alarm — it's a quiet degradation of critical public internet infrastructure that most people won't notice until something breaks. The root cause appears to be funding and prioritization constraints rather than any technical limitation. This is a story about what happens when important but unsexy government-maintained infrastructure gets deprioritized — the CVE/NVD ecosystem is genuinely foundational to how the industry manages security at scale, and its quiet deterioration should be getting more attention than it is.

stories.title: Stripe and Airwallex, Once Close to a Merger, Are Now Direct Competitors
stories.summary: Airwallex and Stripe have historically carved up the fintech market by geography and customer segment — Airwallex started with Asia-Pacific business payments, Stripe dominated US/European developer-friendly payment infrastructure. TechCrunch reports that the two companies were close enough to an acquisition deal at some point that Stripe considered buying Airwallex, but that deal didn't happen, and now they're actively going after each other's customers. This is a meaningful competitive dynamic because Airwallex has matured into a genuinely capable global payments platform and is now targeting the enterprise and mid-market customers that Stripe has built its expansion strategy around. Stripe has simultaneously been pushing harder into international markets where Airwallex has incumbency advantages. The payments infrastructure market is enormous but the premium, developer-friendly segment is more limited — there isn't room for two companies with identical positioning to coexist comfortably. For companies evaluating payments infrastructure: more competition here is probably good for you in the short term as both companies sharpen pricing and features, but vendor consolidation risk is worth thinking about. The acquisition-that-wasn't is an interesting footnote — it suggests both companies recognized the overlap and tried to resolve it corporately before the market forced the issue. Stripe's valuation and IPO trajectory means it needs to show continued expansion into new markets, which puts it on a collision course with Airwallex in Asia-Pacific regardless of how either company positions strategically.

stories.title: Satellite Images Reveal 40% of US Data Centers Planned for 2026 Are Significantly Delayed
stories.summary: A genuinely striking analysis using satellite and drone imagery has found that roughly 40% of US data centers planned for completion in 2026 are facing significant construction delays — driven by a combination of energy grid bottlenecks, supply chain issues for specialized equipment, and growing community resistance to large power-hungry facilities. The energy bottleneck is the most systemic: utility interconnection queues in major data center markets like Northern Virginia, Phoenix, and Dallas have stretched to multi-year timelines, and no amount of construction acceleration helps if you can't get a grid connection. This creates a real constraint on AI infrastructure scaling that the industry's projections have consistently underestimated — hyperscaler capex announcements sound impressive until you account for the gap between 'announced' and 'operational.' The community resistance piece is also growing: local governments and residents are pushing back on data center sprawl for reasons ranging from water usage to property tax impacts to noise. For anyone tracking AI infrastructure capacity: the implication is that the compute scaling story for 2026-2027 is more constrained than headline numbers suggest, which could affect model development timelines and inference costs. Meta's Quest headsets becoming more expensive because AI data center investments are driving up component costs (reported separately this week) is a nice illustration of how these infrastructure constraints ripple into unexpected places. The satellite/drone imagery methodology is clever — it provides ground truth against announced timelines that project developers and investors have every incentive to present optimistically.

stories.title: Hyperscaler Data Center Spending Has Already Outpaced Most Famous US Megaprojects
stories.summary: A widely-shared analysis (164 points on Hacker News) makes the striking point that hyperscalers — Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta — have collectively already outspent most of the famous large-scale US infrastructure and industrial projects used as historical benchmarks for 'big spending.' We're talking about comparisons to the Manhattan Project, the Interstate Highway System, and the Apollo Program in inflation-adjusted terms. The numbers are genuinely staggering: the four major hyperscalers combined are running at annual capex rates that, sustained over even a few years, exceed the real-dollar cost of these historically massive programs. The context matters for two reasons. First, it's a useful corrective to the common framing that AI infrastructure investment is 'just' corporate capex rather than something with transformative physical infrastructure implications. Second, it raises real questions about whether this pace of investment can be sustained and what the return-on-investment story looks like if the AI revenue projections don't materialize as fast as the infrastructure buildout. The data center delay story and this spending story sit in interesting tension — the industry is committing to historically unprecedented infrastructure investment while simultaneously struggling to actually build that infrastructure at the announced pace. For investors and strategists: the capex commitment is real even if the construction timeline is slipping, which means the financial exposure is locked in before the revenue validates it.

stories.title: Amazon Locks Down New Fire Sticks, Blocking Sideloading
stories.summary: Amazon's two newest Fire Stick models ship with sideloading disabled — you can no longer install apps from outside Amazon's official store on the new hardware, a significant change from previous generations that supported Android APK sideloading. This matters for a technically engaged audience because sideloading was the primary way to install Kodi, Plex, and other media apps that compete with or supplement Amazon's own offerings, as well as adblockers and alternative launchers that could strip out Amazon's aggressive content promotion. The move is almost certainly a combination of business protection (keeping you in Amazon's ecosystem and monetization layer) and genuine security reasoning (sideloading is a real attack vector on consumer devices). But the 'security' framing is hard to take entirely seriously given that Amazon's motivation to sell you Prime content is obvious. This follows a broader platform trend — Apple was forced by EU regulation to allow alternative app stores on iOS, but Amazon is going the other direction on its own hardware. Fire Sticks occupy an interesting middle position in the streaming market: they're cheap, they're everywhere, and a meaningful portion of their user base consists of technically sophisticated users who used sideloading extensively. Whether those users will tolerate the locked-down experience or migrate to Roku, Google TV, or other platforms will be an interesting market signal in the next year. For privacy-conscious users: this effectively ends the Fire Stick as a viable platform for privacy-respecting media consumption setups.

stories.title: Hackers Are Actively Exploiting Three Unpatched Windows Defender Vulnerabilities
stories.summary: A security researcher published details and working exploit code for three vulnerabilities in Windows Defender, and attackers wasted essentially no time — cybersecurity firms are now reporting active exploitation of all three in real-world attacks against organizations. The combination of published exploit code and unpatched vulnerabilities is about as bad as it gets from a defensive posture standpoint: the attacker advantage is maximal when both the technique and the tool are public but the patch doesn't exist yet. Windows Defender is particularly sensitive because it runs with elevated privileges and processes untrusted input by design (it has to scan files to detect malware), making it a high-value attack surface. The responsible disclosure debate is implied here — the researcher published without coordinating with Microsoft on patch timing, which is the choice that leads to this situation. For security and IT teams: if you're running Windows environments (which is to say, most corporate environments), this needs to be on your radar for emergency patching the moment Microsoft releases fixes, and you should be watching your Defender logs in the meantime for anomalous behavior. The broader context of NIST stepping back from CVE enrichment (reported separately) makes the timing particularly unfortunate — the tooling organizations use to prioritize vulnerability response is degrading just as active exploitation becomes more aggressive.

stories.title: Grinex (US-Sanctioned Crypto Exchange) Blames 'Unfriendly States' for $15M Hack
stories.summary: Grinex, a cryptocurrency exchange that operates under US sanctions due to connections to Russia-friendly financial networks, is claiming that a $15 million heist was carried out by Western intelligence services — specifically citing that the hacking resources required are 'available exclusively to unfriendly states.' This is the geopolitical version of the classic corporate breach PR move (blame someone else with maximum implausibility), but it's worth parsing seriously because state-level cyber operations against sanctioned financial infrastructure do happen. The irony of a sanctions-evading exchange invoking the language of nation-state attribution to deflect from what might simply be a security failure or an inside job is rich. The substantive question — who actually has both the motivation and capability to drain a sanctioned exchange — has a long answer that includes not just Western intelligence services but also criminal groups, competing criminal organizations, and disgruntled insiders. From a crypto security standpoint, exchanges operating outside regulatory frameworks consistently have weaker security postures because they can't access mainstream security tooling and talent easily. For anyone watching the crypto security landscape: the political theater here is less interesting than the underlying dynamic that sanctioned financial infrastructure is a high-value target for multiple threat actors simultaneously, making attribution genuinely complicated and attribution claims from the victim particularly unreliable.

stories.title: Banning the Sale of Precise Geolocation Data: The Case Is Getting Stronger
stories.summary: A Lawfare piece making the case for banning the sale of precise geolocation data got significant traction on Hacker News (663 points, one of the highest-scored items this week), reflecting both the technical community's long-running frustration with data broker practices and the genuine policy momentum building around this issue. Precise geolocation data — defined as fine-grained location tracking sufficient to infer home address, workplace, places of worship, medical facilities visited, and similar sensitive information — is bought and sold by data brokers with essentially no meaningful oversight in the US. The article makes the case that this isn't just a privacy issue but a national security one: the same data that lets advertisers target you lets foreign intelligence services track US military personnel, identify intelligence officers, and map social networks of sensitive individuals. This argument has been gaining traction in national security circles and is one of the more effective political framings for a privacy regulation that might otherwise get stalled by industry lobbying. For engineers who work in location services, advertising technology, or mobile apps: this represents a real regulatory risk that is closer to materializing than it has been at any prior point. The combination of national security framing, bipartisan concern, and documented harms (including to journalists, domestic violence survivors, and military personnel) creates a policy window. The technical implication is that products built on monetizing precise location data need backup revenue models.

stories.title: Artemis II Pilot Describes What It Was Actually Like to Fly and Land in Orion
stories.summary: The pilot of the Artemis II mission — the first crewed lunar flyby since Apollo 17 in 1972 — gave a detailed account of what the mission actually felt like from inside the Orion capsule, with particular focus on reentry and landing. The quote 'I've been thinking about reentry for three straight years' gives a sense of the psychological weight of piloting a vehicle on a trajectory that hadn't been flown with humans aboard in decades. Reentry for a lunar-return trajectory is significantly more intense than ISS return — the approach angles and speeds are different in ways that have outsized effects on heat shield performance and crew g-forces, and the margin for error is correspondingly smaller. The personal account is a useful complement to the technical reporting that tends to dominate coverage of Artemis milestones — it's a reminder that there are humans inside these systems making real-time decisions. The Artemis program has been expensive and slow by almost any measure, but Artemis II getting humans to lunar distance and back safely is a genuine milestone for the program's credibility. Meanwhile, Starship V3 was test-fired this week and Blue Origin is preparing the third New Glenn flight with a reused booster — the competitive dynamics of the cislunar transportation market are shifting faster than NASA's official program timeline. For space enthusiasts: the pilot's account is worth reading in full as primary source material on what modern deep-space crewed flight actually involves.

stories.title: Stealth Signals Are Bypassing Iran's Total Internet Blackout Via Satellite TV
stories.summary: IEEE Spectrum published a detailed technical investigation into how Iranians have been circumventing the country's near-total internet shutdown — imposed on January 8, 2026, the first complete information blackout in the country's history — using satellite TV signals as a covert data channel. The technique exploits the broadcast nature of satellite TV: signals reach the country regardless of government control over ground-based internet infrastructure, and the unused bandwidth in satellite TV transmissions can carry hidden data streams that are difficult to detect and impossible to block without disabling all satellite reception. This isn't entirely new technology — similar techniques have been used in other censorship contexts — but the scale and sophistication of their deployment in Iran is notable. The technical details in the Spectrum piece are genuinely interesting from an engineering standpoint: the encoding methods, the receiver hardware required, and the throughput achievable through these covert channels. The broader context of Iran AI warfare (referenced in the MIT Technology Review piece this week) adds a layer: the information environment inside Iran is being shaped simultaneously by government blackouts and by these circumvention technologies, which matters for understanding the conflict's dynamics. For engineers interested in adversarial communications: this is a real-world case study in how broadcast physics creates inherent limitations on information control that state actors can't fully overcome with traditional internet infrastructure controls.
